posts.raw_content:
Look, I know this might rustle some feathers among the older neocons still hanging around, but it needs to be said: we are watching a massive geopolitical shift happen right in front of our eyes. I call it "The Great Jewish Fatigue" - and before anyone starts pearl-clutching, I'm talking strictly about the political exhaustion with the Israeli government's endless demands and the domestic lobbying apparatus that pushes for unconditional American blank checks.

For decades, the standard conservative playbook was simple: Israel right or wrong. But the America First base is waking up, and more importantly, the Trump administration is actively reflecting this shift. We are finally seeing a strategic distancing from Israel's most hawkish impulses.

Here is why it's happening and how the administration is signaling that the era of unconditional subservience is over.

TRUMP 47 IS NOT TRUMP 45

During Trump's first term, the administration gave Israel almost everything it wanted (moving the embassy to Jerusalem, the Abraham Accords). But since taking office again in 2025, Trump has been focused on ending endless Middle Eastern conflicts, not funding them.

Look at the current reality in 2026. Trump brokered the multi-phase Gaza peace deal late last year and just recently spearheaded a new US-Iran agreement to stabilize the region and keep global markets from tanking. And how did Netanyahu's government react? By undermining it, keeping troops in southern Lebanon, and complaining. Trump even had to publicly tell Netanyahu to use a softer touch. The administration is clearly getting tired of a supposed ally constantly trying to drag us back into a regional war.

ENTER JD VANCE: THE AMERICA FIRST ATTACK DOG

If you want to see exactly where the populist right stands on this, look no further than Vice President JD Vance. Just this week (June 2026), Vance went on record dropping some brutal truths that no Republican leader would have dared say a decade ago.

Calling out the Freakout: Vance straight-up dismissed Israel's reaction to the US-Iran deal as a weird panic and an odd freakout. He correctly pointed out that America has earned the region's trust and that Israel's inherent mistrust is their problem, not ours.

A Reality Check on the Blank Check: When far-right Israeli ministers like Itamar Ben-Gvir and Bezalel Smotrich started barking about the deal, Vance didn't hold back. He reminded them: "What is your exact proposal? You're a country of 9 million people. You can't just kill your way out of solving every single national security problem that you have."

The Taxpayer Reminder: Vance also made a point to remind Israeli critics that two-thirds of the weapons protecting their homeland are manufactured in the US and paid for by American tax dollars.

WHAT THIS MEANS FOR THE CONSERVATIVE MOVEMENT

Vance's rhetoric isn't an accident; it's a calculated signal. He literally stated: "We also have aligned interests, but also sometimes we're going to disagree on issues from time to time, and I think that's totally reasonable."

That is the definition of a healthy, transactional foreign policy. For too long, questioning the billions of dollars we send overseas or refusing to fight someone else's war was treated as political heresy on the Right.

"The Great Jewish Fatigue" isn't about animosity; it's about a return to realism. It is the realization that Israel's borders are not our borders, and their wars are not our wars. We wish them the best, but when push comes to shove, American resources, American tax dollars, and American lives must come first. Period.
